one_line_takeaway: LESSON is the first framework to craft physically-constrained, multi-label adversarial FDIA perturbations that simultaneously evade Bad Data Detection and per-meter deep-learning attack localization in smart grids, reaching up to 100% white-box evasion on the IEEE 118-bus system.
key_finding: LESSON reliably evades multi-label FDIA localization while staying stealthy to BDD by construction. At small FDIA scale (learning rate 0.001), all four attacks reach 100% success on the 118-bus system; even the hardest LESSON-4 exceeds 60% on 14- and 30-bus systems and most other cases are near 100%. Larger power grids are MORE vulnerable, and larger FDIA scales are harder to hide: for LESSON-2 on 14-bus, success falls 98.73% -> 74.42% -> 24.37% across small/medium/large scales, while the 118-bus system at large scale still achieves 80.52% / 55.19% / 75.32% / 42.21% for LESSON-1..4. Whether the induced estimation error must be preserved (targeted, Objective One) drives difficulty far more than the hide-all-labels choice (Objective Two). Adam's learning rate must be tuned to FDIA scale (larger scale needs larger LR up to a point; Objective-Two-2 cases collapse to 0% if LR is too high). Shrinking the state range from 1 rad to 0.5 rad only mildly reduces success, and even with half the non-mandatory meters made uncontrollable the attack retains meaningful success.
core_contribution: The paper proposes LESSON (muLti-labEl adverSarial falSe data injectiON attack), described by the authors as the first framework for crafting multi-label adversarial examples against deep-learning-based FDIA *locational* detectors in smart grids. Prior adversarial-FDIA work (the authors' own AFDIA [19][20]) targeted only single-label detection (attack present / absent); LESSON extends this to multi-label Neural Attack Location (NAL), where each meter is an independent label. The framework has three components the authors present as their own: (1) Perturbing State Variables - deriving the adversarial perturbation as H*zeta (perturbing state variables x rather than measurements z directly) so that the residual is provably unchanged and Bad Data Detection (BDD) is bypassed by construction, letting them drop the BDD constraint from the optimization; (2) Tailored Loss Function Design - converting the per-meter label constraints into hinge-style regularizers max(0, -Upsilon(zf)) for meters that must read 'attacked' (set A) and max(0, Upsilon(zf)) for meters that must read 'normal' (set B) on the pre-sigmoid logits Upsilon, optimizable with Adam; (3) Change of Variables - reparameterizing zeta = mu*tanh(varpi) to keep the state perturbation within a physical range [-mu, mu] without box constraints. From two objective dimensions (Objective One: whether the originally induced state-estimation error c must stay unchanged -> targeted vs untargeted; Objective Two: hide only the FDIA-attacked meters' labels vs hide ALL meters' labels), they instantiate four concrete attacks LESSON-1 (easiest) through LESSON-4 (hardest, targeted + hide-all-labels). NOTE: the many CNN/GNN/CDBN detectors and multi-label image attacks (refs [8]-[15], [42]-[49]) discussed at length are Related Work, not this paper's contribution.
threat_model: White-box attacker against a cyber-physical smart grid running DC state estimation with both a BDD module and a CNN-based multi-label NAL locational detector. The attacker is assumed to know the power-grid topology/parameters and the NAL model parameters (justified via insider threat, eavesdropping, or database intrusion, and framed as a worst-case/APT upper bound). The attacker controls the meters corresponding to non-zero entries of the FDIA attack vector a = Hc (to inject the initial FDIA) plus additional meters needed to add the adversarial perturbation. Goals: Objective One - tamper with state-estimation results (optionally preserving a precise pre-chosen error to hit specific buses, the 'targeted' case); Objective Two - fool NAL's per-meter localization (hide the attacked meters, or hide all meters so neither BDD nor NAL raises any alarm). Success requires simultaneously evading BDD and NAL under the physical constraint that perturbations map through H and stay within the state range mu. The authors acknowledge the white-box premise and ideal attack-cost assumption are strong; black-box and cost-limited variants are left to future work.
limitations: By the authors' own admission: (1) the attack is white-box (needs NAL parameters) - black-box multi-label AFDIA is left to future work; (2) the attack-cost assumption is idealized (attacker has abundant meter-control resources); (3) although larger grids appeared more attackable, they state they have NO conclusive evidence for a success-rate/grid-size relationship; (4) no defense is proposed or evaluated - single-label defenses (adversarial training, defensive distillation) may not transfer to multi-label, and countermeasures are entirely deferred. Additional reviewer-noted scope limits: only DC state estimation is modeled (AC flagged as future work in a footnote); everything is simulation on synthetic Matpower data with no real grid or real detector; there is no comparison against any external attack baseline - all comparisons are among the four LESSON variants and against clean NAL accuracy; and success is measured on the subset of perfectly-classified samples only (500 per setting).

datasets_used: 30,000 generated measurement samples (15,000 normal + 15,000 FDIA-attacked), split 20,000 train / 10,000 test; loads U(80%,120% baseload), 2% Gaussian noise
FDIA vectors a=Hc generated at three scales via target-state variance ν^2 = 0.02 (small), 0.1 (medium), 0.5 (large)
quantitative_results: Small-scale (LR 0.001): all four LESSON attacks achieve 100% success on the 118-bus system; hardest LESSON-4 exceeds 60% on 14- and 30-bus
118-bus, large FDIA scale: LESSON-1=80.52%, LESSON-2=55.19%, LESSON-3=75.32%, LESSON-4=42.21%
Clean CNN NAL detector accuracies (meter accuracy / row accuracy): 14-bus 99.6%/97.6%, 30-bus 99.4%/96.2%, 118-bus 99.1%/93.1%
Perturbation magnitude ordering: LESSON-1 > LESSON-2 and LESSON-3 > LESSON-4 in rho_c; targeted (error-preserving) variants require larger perturbations on other state variables
mu reduced 1 rad -> 0.5 rad: only minor drop in Psuc; mu=0.01 rad would drop many cases to ~0%
Adam learning rates swept: 0.0005, 0.001, 0.005, 0.01, 0.1, 0.2; recommended range roughly [0.001, 0.1] tuned to FDIA scale
baselines_compared: No external adversarial-attack baseline is compared head-to-head; the paper positions itself as the first multi-label AFDIA and extends the authors' own single-label AFDIA [19][20]
Internal comparison across the four attack variants LESSON-1, LESSON-2, LESSON-3, LESSON-4
Comparison against the clean (un-attacked) CNN NAL locational detectors' accuracy
Ablation comparisons: FDIA scale (small/medium/large), Adam learning rate, state range mu (1 vs 0.5 rad), and attack-cost restriction (Eq. 21, half of non-mandatory meters uncontrollable)
